### Pass 171 - Documentation refinement for small-model replication (2026-05-07)

Direct response to the request that "any coding agent, even
small models, should be able to understand and replicate the
entire project from documentation alone, with simple-to-expert
layered detail." A thorough doc-quality audit ran first to scope
the work; this pass shipped the highest-leverage refinements.

#### What the audit found

- **Doc-stamp staleness on five core docs.** `AGENTS.md` (the
  agent-onboarding entry point) hadn't been re-stamped since
  2026-04-24 — 13 days. `MENTAL_MODEL.md`, `DESIGN_PRINCIPLES.md`,
  `EXTENSION_POINTS.md`, and `CONVENTIONS.md` were each between
  7 and 13 days old. Content was still accurate (re-verified
  against current code); only the freshness stamps had drifted.
- **No single "rebuild PalLLM from docs alone" doc.** The
  knowledge to do this was distributed across
  `AGENTS.md` + `ARCHITECTURE.md` + `CODE_MAP.md` + `INDEX.md`
  + `schemas/README.md` + `PROJECT_NUMBERS.json`. Reasonable
  for a thorough reader; problematic for a small model that
  benefits from a single curated entry point.
- **`COMPLETION.md`'s "what to do" section was a sketch.** Seven
  numbered lines, no per-step expected artifacts, no
  verification commands. Operators and agents reading it could
  understand the arc but not actually execute it.

#### What this pass shipped

**(1) New REPLICATION_KIT (retired Pass 376; see `docs/CODE_MAP.md`).** A single replication
entry point. Contents:

- **60-second TL;DR** — three-layer topology in plain monospace
  with the bridge as filesystem mailbox.
- **Reading order** — eight files in a curated sequence with
  per-file "what it answers" and time estimate. Total: ~30 min
  for full mental model. Files include `agents.json` (1 min),
  `PROJECT_NUMBERS.json` (30 sec), `MENTAL_MODEL.md` (5 min),
  `ARCHITECTURE.md` (10 min), `CODE_MAP.md` (5 min),
  `ROADMAP.md` (5 min), `CONVENTIONS.md` (5 min), six ADRs
  (10 min).
- **Replicating from scratch** — eight commands with
  prerequisites (.NET 10 SDK, PowerShell 7+, Lua 5.4 via UE4SS),
  expected outputs, and verification conditions. Steps walk
  clone → build → test → audit → run sidecar → verify chat
  reply → optional inference wire → optional mod install.
- **Layered detail** — seven "pick by need" pointers
  (lay reader → PITCH; operator → OPERATIONS; contributor →
  CONTRIBUTING; agent → AGENTS; harvester → HARVEST;
  security → PRIVACY + SECURITY; release manager → RELEASE).
- **Where every contract lives** — table mapping each wire
  contract (HTTP API, MCP, bridge inbox, bridge outbox,
  personality packs, install manifest, native proof status,
  agent manifest, verb manifest, project numbers) to its
  authoritative source file and its JSON schema.
- **Hard invariants** — six rules with the gating ADR for
  each.
- **Replication verification** — four commands that confirm
  the replica matches canonical behavior.
- **What this doc deliberately does NOT cover** — explicit
  out-of-scope statement pointing at the right deeper docs.

This is the single doc to give a small model. Reading it
top-to-bottom is sufficient to make a competent change without
opening any other doc first.

**(2) Refreshed `docs/COMPLETION.md` "Clean small steps to
100%" section.** Replaced the previous 7-line sketch with six
fully-fleshed steps. Each step has:

- A name and a roadmap percentage weight
- The exact command to run
- The expected output / artifact location
- A verification condition (what proves the step landed)

| Step | Unlocks | Command |
|---|---|---|
| 1 — capture live Palworld smoke proof | ~6.0% | `pal play; pal native-proof` |
| 2 — confirm the production sampler | ~4.5% | flip kill switch in `main.lua`, observe `production` events |
| 3 — bind the native HUD | ~7.5% | `pal hud-bind` |
| 4 — wire native audio | ~2.0% | enable TTS in config, replace local playback with native call |
| 5 — promote remaining native actions | ~3.0% | implement `recall_pals` + `request_craft_queue` natively |
| 6 — ship the clean-machine release | ~1.5% | `pal package; pal verify; pal proof-bundle`; clean-box smoke |

Total unlocked = 24.5pp (covers the 23.8% remainder with
rounding slack the IMPLEMENTATION_QUEUE doc already
acknowledges).

**(3) Refreshed five stale doc stamps.** `AGENTS.md`,
`MENTAL_MODEL.md`, `DESIGN_PRINCIPLES.md`, `EXTENSION_POINTS.md`,
`CONVENTIONS.md` all now stamped `2026-05-07`. Content
re-verified accurate; only the dates moved.

**(4) `AGENTS.md` TL;DR shortcut to REPLICATION_KIT.** A small
model loading the agent-onboarding doc cold now sees the
replication shortcut in the TL;DR and can follow it directly
without a second hop.

**(5) `INDEX.md` cross-link.** New row pointing at
REPLICATION_KIT for the "Replicate PalLLM from documentation
alone (small-model friendly)" entry.

**(6) `docsCount` rolls `63 -> 64`** to track REPLICATION_KIT's
addition. The Pass 170 auxiliary-counts meta-test picks up the
bump automatically — exactly the drift class that gate exists
to catch.

#### What this does and does not change

This pass does not change any production code, HTTP route, MCP
tool, feature-catalog entry, fallback strategy, test count,
verb count, or runtime behaviour. It changes:

- One new doc (`CODE_MAP.md`)
- One refined section in `COMPLETION.md`
- Five refreshed `Last audited:` stamps
- One TL;DR shortcut in `AGENTS.md`
- One INDEX entry
- `docsCount` 63 → 64
- Audit link-target count 248 → 254 (six new cross-references,
  all resolving)

Headline `76.2%` unchanged. The 23.8% gap is still gated on
live hardware. What changed is the *clarity* of the path
through that gap and the *replicability* of everything that's
already shipped.

Verification: full audit `15/15` PASS at
`artifacts/full-audit/20260507-151146/RESULTS.md`; `dotnet test`
`654 / 654`.

### Pass 170 - Auxiliary counts drift gate + meta-test count correction (2026-05-07)

The pattern continues: each pass that audits a hand-maintained
surface finds real drift. Pass 169 found `38` vs `37` MCP tools.
Pass 170 audited the auxiliary counts that surface in
`pal complete` and `docs/COMPLETION.md` and found another off-by.

#### What the audit found

`scripts/pal-complete.ps1` and `docs/COMPLETION.md` both claimed
`21 meta-tests` in their drift-protection summary lines:

```text
$ pwsh ./pal.ps1 complete
...
  - drift protection: 15 gates + 21 meta-tests + 8 JSON Schemas
```

Live count of `[Test]` attributes in `tests/PalLLM.Tests/MetaTests.cs`:
`23`. Three meta-tests had been added (Pass 167's queue inventory,
Pass 168's verb inventory, Pass 169's MCP inventory) without the
advertised count keeping up. Two surfaces, one drift class.

After Pass 170 itself adds a 24th test, both surfaces now read
`24 meta-tests`.

#### New meta-test

`AuxiliaryCounts_DocsAdrsSchemas_AgreeWithLiveFilesystem_AndCompletionSurfaces`
(`tests/PalLLM.Tests/MetaTests.cs`). Closes the loop on six
count claims that weren't yet gated:

| Claim | Live source | Where it surfaces |
|---|---|---|
| `docsCount` | stamped `*.md` in `docs/` | `PROJECT_NUMBERS.json` |
| `adrsAccepted` | `*.md` in `docs/adr/` with `**Status:** Accepted` | `PROJECT_NUMBERS.json` |
| meta-test count | `[Test]` attributes in `MetaTests.cs` | `pal-complete.ps1` literal |
| meta-test count | (same) | `docs/COMPLETION.md` literal |
| JSON Schema count | `*.schema.json` in `docs/schemas/` | `pal-complete.ps1` literal |
| JSON Schema count | (same) | `docs/COMPLETION.md` literal |

The test parses the `pal-complete.ps1` literal `N gates + M
meta-tests + K JSON Schemas` and the `COMPLETION.md` literal
`` `N` meta-tests + `K` JSON Schemas + `G` ``, then cross-checks
each scalar against the live filesystem.

#### Why this matters

Of the recent passes that audited a hand-maintained surface:

| Pass | Surface | Real drift found |
|------|---------|------------------|
| 162 | aggregate score | 7.7 vs 8.5 (mean 8.0) |
| 163 | Lua / connectors / honest verdict | 5 docs claiming Lua 5.1 |
| 164 | operator scripts | 5 verbs missing from `pal` |
| 168 | verb inventory | `$known` missing 6 verbs |
| 169 | MCP inventory | `38` vs `37` tools |
| **170** | **auxiliary counts** | **`21` vs `23` (now 24) meta-tests** |

Six of the last nine passes found real drift. Each fix became a
permanent meta-test. The pattern: any hand-maintained number that
isn't gated will eventually drift; every audit reveals what the
previous audit didn't reach; every fix shrinks the un-gated
surface area.

#### Test-count rollup

`653 -> 654` (+1: the new auxiliary-counts gate). Bumped across
the same 24 anchor files prior passes touched.

#### What this does and does not change

Pure drift-protection + two stale literal corrections (`21 -> 24`
in `pal-complete.ps1` and `docs/COMPLETION.md`). No production
code, no HTTP route, no MCP tool, no feature-catalog entry, no
fallback strategy. The verb's output and the doc both now match
each other and match the live MetaTests.cs count.

Verification: `dotnet test` `654 / 654`; full audit `15/15` PASS
at `artifacts/full-audit/20260507-143845/RESULTS.md`.

### Pass 169 - MCP inventory drift gate + 38 -> 37 tool count correction (2026-05-07)

The pattern from the recent passes continues: each pass that
audits a hand-maintained surface finds real drift. Pass 162
found arithmetic drift between `agents.json` and `READINESS.md`.
Pass 168 found `$known` had fallen behind the dispatch by six
verbs. Pass 169 audited the MCP-inventory chain.

#### What the audit found

The long-claimed `38 MCP tools` was off-by-one. The live source
file `src/PalLLM.Sidecar/Mcp/PalLlmMcpTools.cs` contains only
`37` method-level `[McpServerTool(Name = "...")]` declarations.
The number had propagated across docs, scripts, `agents.json`,
`PROJECT_NUMBERS.json`, and the README topology diagram added
in Pass 166.

The likely origin of the `38`: counting the class-level
`[McpServerToolType]` decorator alongside the 37 method-level
`[McpServerTool]` attributes. The class-level decorator is a
registration marker — it tells the MCP host "this class
contains tools" — but it's not itself a tool that an MCP client
can invoke. So `1 + 37 = 38` was an off-by-one that nothing
caught because no test compared the live attribute count to
`PROJECT_NUMBERS.json`.

#### What the existing chain checked vs. what it missed

The Pass 157 meta-test
`SelfDescriptionBuilder_HardcodedLiterals_AgreeWith_ProjectNumbers`
pinned `SelfDescriptionBuilder.cs`'s `"38 tools"` literal to
`PROJECT_NUMBERS.json`'s `mcpTools: 38`. Both files said `38`.
The test passed. Both files were wrong.

The chain looked solid:

```
live source (37 tools)
   |
   |  ??? not gated ???
   v
PROJECT_NUMBERS.json mcpTools = 38
   |
   |  Pass 157 meta-test
   v
SelfDescriptionBuilder.cs "38 tools"
   |
   |  not directly gated
   v
docs claim "38 MCP tools"
```

The dotted "not gated" edge from live source to
`PROJECT_NUMBERS.json` is what Pass 169 closes.

#### New meta-test

`McpInventory_LiveAttributeCounts_AgreeWith_ProjectNumbers`
counts each MCP attribute in the live source and asserts every
count agrees with the matching field in `PROJECT_NUMBERS.json`:

| Attribute pattern | PROJECT_NUMBERS field |
|---|---|
| `[McpServerTool]` (method level) | `mcpTools` |
| `[McpServerPrompt(Name = "...")]` | `mcpPrompts` |
| `[McpServerResource(...)]` with simple URI | `mcpResources` |
| `[McpServerResource(UriTemplate = "...{...}...")]` | `mcpResourceTemplates` |

The class-level `[McpServerToolType]` and `[McpServerPromptType]`
decorators are deliberately excluded — they're registration
markers, not tools/prompts.

For resources specifically, the test distinguishes templated
from simple URIs by checking whether the `UriTemplate` argument
contains a `{placeholder}`. Live source has 7 total
`[McpServerResource]` attributes: 6 with simple URIs (`Uri =
"palllm://world/snapshot"`) and 1 with a templated URI (`Uri =
"palllm://character/{characterId}"`). The split matches
`mcpResources: 6` and `mcpResourceTemplates: 1` exactly.

#### Drift correction (38 -> 37)

Source-of-truth files:

- `docs/PROJECT_NUMBERS.json` `mcpTools: 38 -> 37`
- `agents.json` `rollingState.mcpTools: 38 -> 37`
- `src/PalLLM.Sidecar/SelfDescriptionBuilder.cs` literal
  `"38 tools, ..." -> "37 tools, ..."`
- `src/PalLLM.Domain/Runtime/PalLlmFeatureCatalog.cs` Notes
  string referencing `38 tools in PalLlmMcpTools`

Public-facing doc claims:

- `README.md` (3 references including the Pass 166 topology
  diagram and the badge-row claim)
- `AGENTS.md` (TL;DR line)
- `docs/HANDOFF.md` (current audited state)
- `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` (Mermaid graph node)
- `docs/CHEAT_SHEET.md` (file-table row)
- `docs/CODE_MAP.md` (tree comment)
- `docs/COMPLETION.md` (autonomous-progress table row)
- `docs/READINESS.md` (3 references)
- `docs/MCP_QUICKSTART.md` (capability summary)
- AGENTIC_PATTERNS_2026 (retired Pass 418) (2 references)
- `docs/FUTURE_2035.md` (2 references including the
  Tool Search Tool first-deliverable)
- `docs/GLOSSARY.md` (MCP definition)
- `docs/PITCH.md` (player-facing pitch)

Scripts:

- `scripts/package-release.ps1` (release-note template line)

Historical entries left untouched: the previous CHANGELOG
entries and `docs/HANDOFF.md` "what just landed" entries from
prior passes that quote the SelfDescriptionBuilder literal as
documentation of past state. Those entries are historical
artifacts; rewriting them would erase the record.

#### Test-count rollup

`652 -> 653` (+1: the new MCP inventory gate). Bumped across
the same 24 anchor files Pass 168 touched.

#### What this does and does not change

This pass does not change any HTTP route, MCP tool, MCP
resource, MCP prompt, feature-catalog entry, fallback strategy,
or runtime behaviour. It corrects a counting error and adds a
test that prevents the same class of error from recurring.

What this enables: future contributors can add or remove an MCP
primitive with confidence — `dotnet test` will fail loudly the
moment the live source disagrees with `PROJECT_NUMBERS.json`,
naming the exact attribute kind and the live-vs-declared
mismatch.

Verification: `dotnet test` `653 / 653`; full audit `15/15` PASS
at `artifacts/full-audit/20260507-015316/RESULTS.md`.

### Pass 168 - Verb inventory drift gate + suggester repair (2026-05-07)

While auditing the operator surface around `pal <verb>` for any
remaining hand-maintained-list drift, two related problems
surfaced together: a real existing drift in the "did you mean?"
suggester, and the absence of a meta-test gating verb-table
agreement across surfaces. Pass 168 fixes both atomically.

#### Existing drift (now fixed)

The `$known` array at `pal.ps1:1186` is consulted when an unknown
verb is typed:

```text
$ pal verfy
Unknown verb: verfy
Did you mean: pal verify ?
```

The array was hand-maintained against the dispatch table, and six
verbs added in Pass 164 / Pass 165 (`native-proof`, `replay`,
`proof-bundle`, `hud-bind`, `verify`, `complete`) had landed
everywhere except in this list. A typo for any of them — `pal
verfy`, `pal hud-bnd`, `pal complte` — would print "Unknown
verb" with no suggestion. The verb existed; the suggester just
didn't know to suggest it.

All six added. The list now enumerates 54 verbs, agreeing with
the dispatch and the manifest.

#### New drift gate

Added `Pal_VerbInventory_AgreesAcross_PalJson_PalPs1Dispatch_RunList`
in `tests/PalLLM.Tests/MetaTests.cs`. Parses four surfaces and
asserts they enumerate the same verb set:

| # | Surface | What it is |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `pal.ps1` top-level dispatch | the canonical "what does `pal <verb>` actually run?" `switch ($Verb.ToLowerInvariant())` block |
| 2 | `pal.json` `groups[].verbs[].verb` | machine-readable verb manifest read by any agent |
| 3 | `pal.ps1` `Run-List` table | what `pal list` prints — every `Verb = '<name>'` entry across all groups |
| 4 | `pal.ps1` `$known` array | the "did you mean?" suggester's closest-match candidates |

Each surface has 54 entries today. The test extracts the four
sets, computes pairwise diffs against the dispatch (the source
of truth), and emits a precise message naming which surface is
missing which verb if any diff is non-empty.

Catches the failure mode this pass found (`$known` falling
behind) and any future variant: someone adds a `pal frobnicate`
to the dispatch but forgets to add it to `pal.json`; someone
removes a deprecated verb from the dispatch but leaves it in
`Run-List`; someone tweaks the suggester's array without
updating the test. Each scenario fails `dotnet test` with the
exact diff named.

#### What this does and does not change

This pass does not change any production code, HTTP route, MCP
tool, feature-catalog entry, fallback strategy, doc count, or
verb count. It changes:

- Six new entries in the `pal.ps1` `$known` suggester array
- One new NUnit test (`MetaTests.cs`)
- 24 test-count anchor files bumped from `651` to `652`

What this enables: every `pal` verb is now surfaced consistently
across all four surfaces operators or agents might consult, and
the next contributor to add or remove a verb cannot silently
leave one surface stale.

Verification: `dotnet test` `652 / 652`; full audit `15/15` PASS
at `artifacts/full-audit/20260507-012838/RESULTS.md`.

### Pass 167 - Queue inventory drift gate (2026-05-06)

Pass 165 shipped two completion surfaces — `pal complete`
(`scripts/pal-complete.ps1`) and `docs/COMPLETION.md` — each
rendering the same six queues from
`docs/IMPLEMENTATION_QUEUE.md`. Three surfaces, one inventory.
The hand-off comment in the previous pass even noted this as a
class of drift to watch:

> Pass 165 also does not advance the headline 76.2%. That number
> reflects phase completion per ROADMAP.md's player-experience-
> weighted scoring [...] What Pass 165 does is make the operator's
> path through that 23.8% visible in one verb.

What it didn't do was pin the verb's queue list to the doc's
queue table. Without a gate, the next contributor to bump a
queue percentage in one surface could silently leave the other
two stale. The operator running `pal complete` would see one
percentage; the same operator opening `docs/COMPLETION.md` to
verify would see another. Pass 167 closes that drift class with
a single new meta-test on the same pattern as Pass 163's
`LuaVersion_ConsistentAcrossDocs`,
`ConnectorInventory_PalJson_AgentsJson_AndFilesystem_Agree`,
and `HonestVerdict_AgentsJson_AgreesWithReadiness`.

#### `QueueInventory_PalComplete_CompletionMd_ImplementationQueue_Agree`

Added at `tests/PalLLM.Tests/MetaTests.cs:271`. Parses three
sources, asserts they agree:

| Source | What's parsed |
|---|---|
| `scripts/pal-complete.ps1` | the queue-table literal — six (Id, Name, Pct) triples |
| `docs/COMPLETION.md` | the queue-status table — six numbered rows with name + percentage |
| `docs/IMPLEMENTATION_QUEUE.md` | six `## Queue N:` headings plus six "Roadmap value unlocked: about X.X%" lines |

Cross-checks:

- IDs are `Q1..Q6` in script + table.
- Names match byte-equivalent between the script and the doc
  table. (For example: both must say "Compat proof + real
  Palworld smoke" — not "Compat proof + smoke" in one and the
  full phrase in the other.)
- Percentages match after normalising surface conventions
  (`~6.0%`, `about 6.0%`, and `6.0%` all parse to the same value).
- Per-queue percentages also match between the script and
  the IMPLEMENTATION_QUEUE.md `Roadmap value unlocked` lines —
  so the script can't drift from the upstream source of truth
  either.

What the test deliberately does **not** check:

- The IMPLEMENTATION_QUEUE.md headings are more formal than
  the rendered short names (`Compatibility proof + real
  Palworld smoke foundation` vs `Compat proof + real Palworld
  smoke`). The test asserts six headings exist and matches
  count, but does not insist the heading text equal the short
  name. That's the right boundary: upstream is allowed to be
  more formal; the two rendered surfaces have to agree with
  each other.
- The "Definition of 100%" criterion list at the bottom of
  IMPLEMENTATION_QUEUE.md is not parsed — it's textual
  prose that describes outcomes, not a structured inventory.

#### Bug surfaced during test development

The first regex draft for the script's queue-table literal
used a multiline pattern that didn't account for the leading
indent inside the array literal. The test ran clean against
the live file (six queues found) but would have silently
passed if the script's indentation drifted. Final regex
anchors on the Id field followed by Name and Pct on
subsequent lines so the structural shape of the queue table
is the actual gate, not the indentation.

#### Test-count rollup

`650 -> 651` (+1). Bumped across the same 24 anchor files
Pass 163 / Pass 164 touched: `README.md`, `docs/ROADMAP.md`,
`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`, `docs/CODE_MAP.md`, `docs/HANDOFF.md`,
`agents.json`, `pal.json`, `pal.ps1`, `docs/CHEAT_SHEET.md`,
`docs/READINESS.md`, `docs/FAQ.md`, `docs/COOKBOOK.md`,
`docs/REVIEW_CHECKLIST.md`, AGENT_NATIVE (retired Pass 376; see `AGENTS.md`),
`docs/TESTING.md`, FIRST_HOUR (retired Pass 376; see `docs/QUICKSTART.md`), `docs/INVARIANTS.md`,
`.github/copilot-instructions.md`, `CONTRIBUTING.md`,
`CLAUDE.md`, `tests/README.md`, `scripts/onboard.ps1`,
`docs/PROJECT_NUMBERS.json`, this `CHANGELOG.md`.

#### What this does and does not change

This pass does not change any production code, HTTP route, MCP
tool, feature-catalog entry, fallback strategy, doc count, or
verb count. It changes only:

- One new NUnit test (`MetaTests.cs:271`)
- 24 test-count anchor files

It does not advance the headline `76.2%` (still requires live
hardware). It does close a class of drift that future passes
would have hit eventually.

What this enables: a future contributor refactoring the queue
inventory in any one of the three surfaces will see the test
fail with a precise diff message naming the queue Id, the
script name, the doc name, and the disagreement — so the
contributor knows immediately which two surfaces drifted
without eyeballing all three by hand.

Verification: `dotnet test` `651 / 651`; full audit `15/15` PASS
at `artifacts/full-audit/20260506-225736/RESULTS.md`.

### Pass 166 - README topology diagram (Discovery 8 -> 9) (2026-05-06)

`docs/READINESS.md`'s "What I can move autonomously" table names
five aspect-score levers a coding agent can push from the repo
without live hardware:

- Documentation `9 -> 10` (dashboard "5 docs" dropdown)
- Polish `7.7 -> 8` (dashboard CSS / animations / theme refresh)
- Discovery `8 -> 9` (ASCII-diagram screenshots in README)
- Configure `7 -> 8` (already shipped, Pass 105)
- Customize `6 -> 7` (already shipped, Pass 106)

Pass 166 takes the highest-leverage atomic option of the three
remaining: a single topology ASCII diagram embedded in the README
right where readers form their first impression. Every reader,
every clone, every GitHub view sees it.

#### What landed

A monospace box-drawing diagram inserted between the existing
"How it works (one paragraph)" prose and the status block. Three
stacked layers showing the three independent crash domains:

1. **Palworld + UE4SS Lua mod** with the `main.lua` <-> native
   HUD / audio / action-executor render path
2. **PalLLM sidecar** (.NET 10 LTS, ASP.NET Core, cross-platform)
   with the visible loop `InboxWorker -> Runtime -> /api/chat
   -> Outbox` and the major sub-systems labelled
   (`FallbackBehaviorEngine (19)`, `MemoryStream + Relationships`,
   `NarrativePackService (packs)`, `Vision / TTS / ActionExecutor`,
   `/api/* (55) + /mcp (38 tools)`, plus the dashboard URL)
3. **Local inference** (any OpenAI-compatible endpoint) with the
   `pal connect <target>` one-verb wiring path

Connection edges show payload shape (`Bridge/Inbox/*`,
`Bridge/Outbox/*`, `HTTP, OpenAI-compatible`) so the reader sees
the data-flow contract in the same glance as the topology.

A two-sentence postscript anchors the architectural punch lines:

> Three independent processes, three independent crash domains.
> Inference is optional; when it's off the deterministic director
> still produces a competent reply. When it's on, the same path
> just splices the model's output through the same presentation
> plan. The bridge is one-way + advisory: the sidecar never
> reaches into Palworld; the mod consumes outbox files at its own
> cadence.

#### Public-copy gate caught the first draft

The initial diagram named third-party inference brands directly
in the bottom layer ("ollama · llama.cpp · LM Studio · vLLM ·
vLLM-Omni · TensorRT-LLM · OpenVINO · transformers · Foundry").
The `Drift_Public_copy` audit gate blocks brand names of
third-party model providers and clients in publication-facing
files — the README is one of those files. The first audit run
flagged "Found: ollama" as a `[blocked-brand]` issue.

Rewrite to brand-neutral: "any OpenAI-compatible endpoint", "9
first-party connectors covering CPU / GPU / NPU paths and
self-hosted multimodal lanes", with discovery routed through
`pwsh ./pal.ps1 connect` (which emits the brand-named target
list at runtime). The brand-aware list lives in
[`READINESS.md`](docs/READINESS.md) row 5, which is not a
public-facing file under the gate.

This is a useful design pattern: the static README stays
brand-neutral; the runtime verbs and internal docs do the
brand-naming. Drift gates enforce the boundary.

#### What this does and does not change

Pure README addition. No production code, no tests, no verbs,
no doc count change, no `PROJECT_NUMBERS.json` change. The
`Drift_Doc_freshness` gate stays green because the README
already carries a current `Last audit: 2026-05-06` stamp. The
`Drift_Dangling_markdown_links` gate stays green at 247 unique
link targets — the diagram introduces no new links.

What this does change: the casual reader's first-glance mental
model. A user landing on the GitHub README without reading
prose now sees the architecture in one paragraph-sized block.

Verification: full audit `15/15` PASS at
`artifacts/full-audit/20260506-222051/RESULTS.md`.

### Pass 165 - Canonical "are we 100% complete?" surface (2026-05-06)

Pass 164 closed the autonomous portion of completion: every
queue-gating script became a single `pal` verb. Pass 165 takes
the next step — operationalizing the question "are we 100%
complete?" itself. Today the answer is scattered across four
docs (`ROADMAP.md`'s phase percentages, `IMPLEMENTATION_QUEUE.md`'s
six queues with their `Definition of 100%`, `READINESS.md`'s
23-aspect scorecard, `HANDOFF.md`'s "what just landed" log).
A user or agent who asks "where are we?" has to read all four
and synthesize. Pass 165 makes the synthesis explicit and
durable, in two surfaces.

#### New doc: `docs/COMPLETION.md`

Single canonical written surface. Sections:

- **TL;DR** with the verdict — `76.2%` complete by phase, with
  the remaining `23.8%` named explicitly as live-Palworld +
  clean-machine work that no autonomous coding agent can advance.
- **Six-queue status table** with each queue's name, percentage
  weight, current status, and the next command for the live
  operator.
- **Definition of 100%** — the same six criteria from
  `IMPLEMENTATION_QUEUE.md`'s closing section, each mapped to its
  gating queue so readers see the "what does done look like?"
  answer in one row each.
- **Autonomous-progress table** — every dimension a coding agent
  can move from the repo, scored against the `READINESS.md`
  scorecard, with the privacy / supply-chain `10/10` aspects
  highlighted alongside the autonomous-bound aspects at their
  ceiling.
- **"What completing the program means in practice"** —
  step-by-step walk-through for the live operator (7 commands
  Q1->Q6) and the coding-agent perspective (the autonomous
  portion is done; new ideas belong in `COMPANION_INTELLIGENCE.md`
  or `FUTURE_2035.md`).

The doc explicitly does NOT inflate the headline beyond what
`ROADMAP.md` reports; it's a consolidation surface, not a new
scoring system.

#### New verb: `pal complete`

Live terminal-rendered version of the same board. Read-only.
Probes:

- `docs/PROJECT_NUMBERS.json` — rolling counts (test count,
  drift gates, honest roadmap %)
- `/api/bridge/proof` (if sidecar reachable) — live Queue 1
  evidence; falls back to
  `Runtime/ReleaseEvidence/latest-native-proof.json` if the
  sidecar is offline
- latest `artifacts/full-audit/<ts>/RESULTS.md` — latest
  pass / fail status

Then prints:

- **Header line** — honest roadmap %, remaining %, test count,
  drift gates, readiness score, latest audit verdict
- **Queue-by-queue board** — six rows, each with status colour
  (green = PROVEN, yellow = PARTIAL, dark gray = PENDING) and
  the next operator command
- **Definition of 100%** — same six criteria as the doc, mapped
  to gating queues
- **Autonomous progress** — four-line summary of the ceilings
  that have been reached
- **Recommended next command** — single line, advances the
  topmost PENDING queue. When Q1 captures `delivery_proven`,
  the verb auto-promotes Q1 to `PROVEN` and the recommendation
  rolls to Q2.

`-Json` mode emits a structured snapshot suitable for piping
into the dashboard or CI smoke checks. Every queue, every
scalar, the bridge proof status, and the recommended action
all exported.

#### Why a new verb? (vs reusing existing surfaces)

PalLLM already has five state-introspection verbs. Each answers
a different question:

| Verb | Answers |
|------|---------|
| `pal next` | "what single action should I take right now?" |
| `pal proof` | "is Queue 1's native delivery proven?" |
| `pal preflight` | "am I ready to play in 12 checks?" |
| `pal status` | "what are the current counts?" |
| `pal readiness` | "how do all 23 aspects score on 10/10?" |
| `pal complete` | "where am I on the full Q1-Q6 arc to 100%?" |

The new verb fills a real gap — none of the others show the full
queue arc with per-queue next commands.

#### Distinct from `docs/COMPLETION.md`?

Yes:

- `docs/COMPLETION.md` is the **canonical written answer** for a
  reader (a contributor, an agent, a release manager).
- `pal complete` is the **live runtime answer** for an operator
  who's actively advancing queues. Auto-promotes status as
  evidence lands.

Either one alone would only answer half the question. Both
ship.

#### Drift / count rollups

- `docs/PROJECT_NUMBERS.json` `docsCount` `62 -> 63` (added
  `docs/COMPLETION.md`).
- `docs/INDEX.md` gains a "Get the canonical 'are we 100%
  complete?' answer in one doc" entry pointing at the new doc
  + the live verb.
- `pal.ps1` + `pal.json` add the `complete` verb to the Set up
  group.
- `Drift_Dangling_markdown_links` now checks `247` unique link
  targets (was 246; new doc + new INDEX entry point).
- `Drift_Doc_freshness` now checks `64` docs (was 63).

#### What this does and does not change

This pass does not change any production code, HTTP route, MCP
tool, feature-catalog entry, fallback strategy, or test count.
It changes only:

- Operator surface: one new `pal` verb (`complete`)
- Documentation surface: one new doc (`COMPLETION.md`)
- `docsCount` anchor (62 -> 63)
- `INDEX.md` cross-reference

Critically, this pass also does not advance the headline
`76.2%`. That number reflects phase completion per
`ROADMAP.md`'s player-experience-weighted scoring — and the
remaining 23.8% is genuinely live-hardware work. What Pass 165
does is make the operator's path through that 23.8% visible
in one verb.

Verification: `dotnet test` `650 / 650`; full audit `15/15` PASS
at `artifacts/full-audit/20260506-220356/RESULTS.md`.

### Pass 164 - Operator turnkey verbs for Queue 1 / 3 / 6 (2026-05-06)

`docs/IMPLEMENTATION_QUEUE.md` ordered the remaining `23.8%` of the
roadmap as six queues. Each queue gates on at least one PowerShell
script the operator must run — either against a live Palworld
session (Queues 1-5) or against a clean machine (Queue 6). The
scripts existed and were battle-tested. What was missing was a `pal`
verb for five of them, so operators had to remember script paths
instead of typing one verb each. Pass 164 closes that friction gap.

#### New verbs

| Verb | Backed by | Queue | What it does |
|------|-----------|-------|--------------|
| `pal native-proof` | `scripts/run-native-proof.ps1` | 1 | live-Palworld watcher; polls `/api/bridge/proof` until `delivery_proven` and persists `latest-native-proof.json` |
| `pal replay` | `scripts/run-delivery-replay.ps1` | 1 | replays a captured delivery envelope through the live bridge loop |
| `pal hud-bind` | `scripts/apply-hud-bind-recommendation.ps1` | 3 | writes the ranked HUD bind recommendation into `config/native-hud.lua` |
| `pal proof-bundle` | `scripts/export-release-proof-bundle.ps1` | 1 | packages bridge proof + smoke artifact + native-proof artifact + HUD config into one durable validation bundle |
| `pal verify` | `scripts/verify-release-package.ps1` | 6 | verifies a packaged release zip / dir: required files, hashes, publish flags, publication-surface hygiene |

All five follow the existing thin-wrapper pattern: forward
`$script:ForwardArgs` and propagate `$LASTEXITCODE`. The `pal list`
table surfaces them under Operate (4) + Publish (1) so they appear
in the right group when a first-time reader scans the verb table.
`pal.json`'s machine-readable verb manifest mirrors the same five
entries with their `script` pointers.

#### New drift gate

`OperatorTurnkeyScripts_AreEachExposedAsAPalVerb` (added at
`tests/PalLLM.Tests/MetaTests.cs:271`) pins the entire
Queue-aligned turnkey path against future regression. Test design:

1. Define 9 `(scriptRelativePath, expectedPalVerb, queueAnchor)`
   tuples covering every operator-facing roadmap-gating script.
2. For each tuple: assert the script file exists, the verb appears
   in `pal.json`'s manifest pointing at that script, and `pal.ps1`'s
   switch-statement dispatch table has a matching case.
3. Failure messages name the queue anchor so the next contributor
   reading a red test understands which roadmap step the missing
   verb gates.

Catches the failure mode this pass found (5 verbs missing for 5
queue-gating scripts) and any future variant — renaming a script,
deleting a verb, or removing a `pal.json` entry.

#### Aggregate-score drift fix

`pal.ps1:758` had a stale `Honest aggregate: ~8.5 / 10` headline
from before Pass 162 corrected the arithmetic to `~8.0`. The
Pass 163 `HonestVerdict_AgentsJson_AgreesWithReadiness` meta-test
checks `agents.json` and `READINESS.md` but not `pal.ps1`. Bumped
manually so all three surfaces agree. (Extending the meta-test to
cover `pal.ps1` is left as future work — the third surface uses a
`Write-Host` literal that's harder to anchor on cleanly than
JSON / Markdown headlines.)

#### Test-count rollup

`649 -> 650` (+1). Bumped across the same 24 anchor files Pass 163
touched: `README.md`, `docs/ROADMAP.md`, `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`,
`docs/CODE_MAP.md`, `docs/HANDOFF.md`, `agents.json`, `pal.json`,
`pal.ps1`, `docs/CHEAT_SHEET.md`, `docs/READINESS.md`,
`docs/FAQ.md`, `docs/COOKBOOK.md`, `docs/REVIEW_CHECKLIST.md`,
AGENT_NATIVE (retired Pass 376; see `AGENTS.md`), `docs/TESTING.md`, FIRST_HOUR (retired Pass 376; see `docs/QUICKSTART.md`),
`docs/INVARIANTS.md`, `.github/copilot-instructions.md`,
`CONTRIBUTING.md`, `CLAUDE.md`, `tests/README.md`,
`scripts/onboard.ps1`, `docs/PROJECT_NUMBERS.json`, this `CHANGELOG.md`.

#### What this does and does not change

This pass does not change any production code, HTTP route, MCP
tool, feature-catalog entry, or fallback strategy. It changes only
the operator surface: five new `pal` verbs that map 1:1 onto
existing scripts, plus one new meta-test that pins the mapping.

What this means for "completing the program 100% in optimal
order": the autonomous part of the optimal order — making every
queue-gating script a single command for the operator who has
to run it against live hardware — is now fully wired. Closing
the remaining 23.8% still requires a live Palworld session
(Queues 1-5) or a clean machine (Queue 6); those steps cannot be
done autonomously. But every command the operator needs at each
step is now `pal <verb>`, not `powershell -File scripts/...`.

Verification: `dotnet test` `650 / 650`; full audit `15/15` PASS at
`artifacts/full-audit/20260506-213407/RESULTS.md`.

### Pass 163 - Manual sweeps promoted to automated meta-tests (2026-05-06)

Passes 156 through 162 each found a real cross-document drift that the
existing 15 drift gates didn't catch. The fix in each pass was a
one-shot manual sweep; the next time someone introduces the same class
of drift, the audit still won't fire. Pass 163 closes that loop by
turning three of the recurring manual checks into NUnit meta-tests, so
the same class of drift fails `dotnet test` the moment it's
introduced.

- **`LuaVersion_ConsistentAcrossDocs`** (added at
  `tests/PalLLM.Tests/MetaTests.cs:121`).
  Reads `.luacheckrc` as the canonical source of truth for which Lua
  dialect the UE4SS bridge targets. Asserts the same version string
  (currently `5.4`) appears in:
  - `.github/workflows/lua.yml` (CI luacheck pin)
  - `agents.json` (`runtime.lua`)
  - `CLAUDE.md` (Project block)
  - `mod/README.md` (toolchain section)
  - `docs/MENTAL_MODEL.md` (runtime topology)
  - `docs/PROJECT_NUMBERS.json` (`lua` field)

  Catches the failure mode Pass 158 found, where the toolchain pinned
  Lua 5.4 (UE4SS's current public-release runtime) but five docs still
  claimed 5.1.

- **`ConnectorInventory_PalJson_AgentsJson_AndFilesystem_Agree`**
  (added at `tests/PalLLM.Tests/MetaTests.cs:162`).
  Enumerates `scripts/connect-*.ps1` filenames (with
  `connect-mcp-client.ps1` explicitly excluded — that one wires an
  MCP host, not an inference connector). Reads the `connect` verb's
  `scripts` array from `pal.json`. Reads the `inferenceWiring`
  capability map keys from `agents.json`. Asserts all three sets are
  identical.

  Catches the failure mode Pass 161 found, where `pal.json` was
  missing `llamacpp` (the script existed and `pal.ps1`'s switch
  routed it correctly, but the verb's advertised script list was
  short) and `agents.json` was missing `vllm-omni` and
  `transformers`. The 9 connectors are now pinned across all three
  surfaces: `ollama`, `llamacpp`, `lmstudio`, `vllm`, `vllm-omni`,
  `transformers`, `tensorrt`, `openvino`, `foundry`.

- **`HonestVerdict_AgentsJson_AgreesWithReadiness`** (added at
  `tests/PalLLM.Tests/MetaTests.cs:240`).
  Parses `agents.json`'s `honestVerdict.aggregateScore` field
  (currently `"~8.0 / 10"`). Parses the headline sentence in
  `docs/READINESS.md` ("Aggregate honest score: ~8.0 / 10 across 23
  aspects"). Asserts both surfaces reference the same scalar.

  Catches the failure mode Pass 162 found, where the two surfaces
  disagreed by 0.8 (agents.json said `~7.7`, README said `~8.5`)
  for an unknown number of passes — and where the actual arithmetic
  mean of the per-aspect column was `8.034`, agreeing with neither.

#### Bugs fixed during test development

- **`HonestVerdict` regex anchor too tight.** First draft anchored
  the score capture group with `\*\*` immediately after, but the
  README's sentence wraps the whole `**Aggregate honest score: ~8.0
  / 10 across 23 aspects.**` in bold — the closing markers come
  after the trailing prose, not directly after the score. Anchor
  removed.
- **`ConnectorInventory` regex missed `.ps1` extension.** First
  draft matched `pal connect <target>`, but `agents.json`'s command
  strings are written as `pwsh ./pal.ps1 connect <target>` with the
  full extension. Pattern updated to
  `\bpal(?:\.ps1)?\s+connect\s+(?<target>\S+)`.

#### Test-count rollup

`646 -> 649` (+3). The test-count anchor was bumped across the 24
files where it appears (`README.md`, `docs/ROADMAP.md`,
`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`, `docs/CODE_MAP.md`, `docs/HANDOFF.md`,
`agents.json`, `pal.json`, `pal.ps1`, `docs/CHEAT_SHEET.md`,
`docs/READINESS.md`, `docs/FAQ.md`, `docs/COOKBOOK.md`,
`docs/REVIEW_CHECKLIST.md`, AGENT_NATIVE (retired Pass 376; see `AGENTS.md`),
`docs/TESTING.md`, FIRST_HOUR (retired Pass 376; see `docs/QUICKSTART.md`), `docs/INVARIANTS.md`,
`.github/copilot-instructions.md`, `CONTRIBUTING.md`, `CLAUDE.md`,
`tests/README.md`, `scripts/onboard.ps1`, `docs/PROJECT_NUMBERS.json`,
this `CHANGELOG.md`).

#### What this does and does not catch

Drift that *will* fire the new gates: bumping the Lua runtime
without updating the docs, adding a tenth connector script without
wiring `pal.json` and `agents.json`, scoring a new aspect in
`READINESS.md` without recomputing `agents.json`.

Drift that *won't* fire (so the older manual sweeps still matter):
hardcoded counts in `SelfDescriptionBuilder.cs` already have a
dedicated meta-test from Pass 157; schema-file existence is checked
by `Drift_Schema_files_exist`; and per-doc freshness is enforced by
`Drift_Doc_freshness` at 45 days. The three new gates fill the
remaining holes the prior manual sweeps surfaced.

No production-code changes. No new dependencies. No new HTTP routes,
MCP tools, or feature-catalog entries. Pure test-suite expansion
that turns three recurring manual sweeps into permanent guardrails.

Verification: `dotnet test` `649 / 649`; full audit `15/15` PASS at
`artifacts/full-audit/20260506-211329/RESULTS.md`.

### Pass 162 - Aggregate score arithmetic fix (2026-05-06)

The "fifth-angle sweep — numerical consistency" pass. Four prior deep-sweep
passes (156, 157, 158, 161) covered source-tree, hardcoded-literal, schema,
connector, and forward-doc drift. This pass targeted numerical claims that
ought to agree but had silently diverged.

- **Aggregate-score disagreement between two sources.**
  `agents.json:252` claimed `aggregateScore: "~7.7 / 10"` while
  `docs/READINESS.md:28` claimed `"Aggregate honest score: ~8.5 / 10"`.
  Both purported to summarise the same 23-aspect scorecard. Computing
  the arithmetic mean of the per-aspect column in READINESS:

  ```
  8 + 7 + 9.5 + 7 + 9 + 5 + 8 + 9.7 + 9.5 + 8.5 + 9 + 9.8 +
  7.5 + 7.7 + 9 + 4 + 3 + 6 + 8.5 + 10 + 10 + 9.2 + 9.9 = 184.8
  184.8 / 23 = 8.034
  ```

  Neither claim was correct. Both fixed to `~8.0 / 10` (the computed
  value, rounded to one decimal).

- **Band-distribution claim was internally inconsistent.** The README
  said:
  ```
  2 aspects 10/10 + 14 aspects 8-9.7/10
                  + 4 aspects 6-7.5/10
                  + 3 aspects 3-5/10
                  = 23
  ```
  Actual counts by band:
  ```
  2 aspects 10/10        ✓
  2 aspects 9.8-9.9/10   (was missing from the breakdown)
  11 aspects 8-9.7/10    (claimed 14)
  5 aspects 6-7.7/10     (claimed 4 in 6-7.5; #14 at 7.7 was orphaned)
  3 aspects 3-5/10       ✓
  Total: 23              ✓
  ```
  Bands rebalanced so the breakdown sums correctly with no orphan rows.

- **Defensive comment removed.** The old text read "Pass 111 nudged
  Performance-Blackwell 9.7 -> 9.8 with the 2026 multimodal stack but
  left the headline aggregate flat -- one +0.1 across 23 aspects rounds
  below visibility." This was post-hoc justification for a +0.5
  inflation in the headline that didn't match the math. Replaced with
  "the doc reports the computed value, not an aspirational headline" so
  the next contributor doesn't carry the inflation forward.

- **Cross-check sentence added** so future readers can verify the
  arithmetic in one line: "the per-aspect column sums to 184.8,
  average = 8.034 ≈ 8.0".

No code changes. No new dependencies. Pure metadata correction that
brings two cross-document numerical claims into agreement with the
arithmetic that defines them.

Verification: `dotnet test` `646 / 646`; full audit `15/15` PASS at
`artifacts/full-audit/20260506-203557/RESULTS.md`.

### Pass 161 - Connector inventory consistency + FUTURE_2035 horizon doc (2026-05-06)

The "fourth-angle drift sweep + 2030+ design horizon" pass. Three prior
deep-sweep passes (156, 157, 158) closed source-tree, hardcoded-literal,
and schema drift. This pass targets cross-references between the
nine-inference-connector surface that grew over the prior agent's overnight
work, plus consolidates the project's forward-looking architecture into
one referencable doc.

**Connector inventory drift fixes:**

- **`pal.json` `connect` verb scripts array missed `connect-llamacpp.ps1`.**
  All other surfaces (`pal.ps1`'s `Run-Connect` switch, `agents.json`,
  `CODE_MAP.md` newly-added connector block, the live filesystem) listed
  it; only `pal.json` was 8-of-9. Fixed.
- **`pal.json` `pack` verb summary still read "manage personality
  packs (list/new)"** with the `scripts` array missing
  `pal-pack-copy.ps1` even after Pass 156 fixed `pal.ps1`'s `Run-List`
  description. Both bumped to include `copy <name>` + the script
  path.
- **`agents.json` `inferenceWiring` capability map missed `vllm-omni`
  and `transformers`.** Listed only 7 of 9 connectors. Added rows for
  `vllmOmniMultimodal` and `transformersServe` with their canonical
  command lines, plus a cross-link to `MULTIMODAL_RECIPES.md`.
- **`CODE_MAP.md` connector listing was inconsistent** -- five
  newer connectors (`llamacpp`, `lmstudio`, `foundry`, `openvino`,
  `tensorrt`) were buried under "HTTP + MCP machinery" while the
  other four (`ollama`, `vllm`, `vllm-omni`, `transformers`) weren't
  listed at all. New dedicated `### Inference-engine connectors`
  block lists every connector with its file path + verb path,
  consistent shape across all nine.

**New doc: `docs/FUTURE_2035.md`** -- horizon-scan companion to
`ROADMAP.md`. ROADMAP is the build queue weighted by player-experience;
FUTURE_2035 is the design horizon that captures cutting-edge and 2030+
ideas with clear architectural fit. Each idea includes:

- Where it slots into the existing PalLLM architecture (named file +
  seam).
- The smallest first deliverable (a runnable PR-sized slice).
- What blocks it today (so the project doesn't re-derive the
  rejection).
- A hard-rule check against the four invariants
  (deterministic-first, observer-only, filesystem-only bridge,
  opt-in everything).

15 ideas across three time horizons:

- **Near-term (12-18 months):** Tool Search Tool meta-MCP surface,
  three-tier memory graph (working / recall / archival), speculative
  companion replies, sleep-mode dreaming (idle-tier reflection),
  per-companion LoRA hot-swap.
- **Mid-term (3-5 years):** Pyramid Mixture-of-Agents router,
  Programmatic Tool Calling sandbox, Constitutional fallback
  critique, authored-strategy compilable promotion, voice-cloning
  with consent gating.
- **Far-term (5-10 years, post-2030):** Federated companion
  identity (signed `.companion` export), auditable companion
  soul-state timeline, multi-agent companion mesh, self-contained
  on-device LoRA fine-tuning, diffusion-based scene narration.

Plus a **"Hard no's"** section documenting five recurring ideas
deliberately rejected (auto-acting companion, sidecar reaching into
Palworld's process, cloud-required inference, auto-applying
promotions to source, persistent telemetry without consent) with the
ADR each one violates, so future contributors don't re-derive the
rejection.

`docs/PROJECT_NUMBERS.json` `docsCount` rolls `61 -> 62`;
`docs/INDEX.md` gains a one-line cross-link entry under the
agent-doc group describing the new horizon doc.

No code changes. Pure metadata + design-direction
consolidation. The connector surface is now internally consistent
across five sources of truth (filesystem / pal.ps1 router /
pal.json manifest / agents.json capabilities / CODE_MAP) and the
project's forward-looking design has a single referencable
horizon doc instead of being scattered across pass entries.

Verification: `dotnet test` `646 / 646`;
`Drift_Dangling_markdown_links` PASS (246 link targets check, was
238 -> 246 from the new doc's 8 cross-links, all resolve);
`Drift_Doc_freshness` PASS (new doc's 2026-05-06 stamp); full
audit `15/15` PASS at
`artifacts/full-audit/20260506-202103/RESULTS.md`.

### Pass 160 - llama.cpp connector + current GGUF proof flags (2026-05-06)

Focused local-model pass closing the raw GGUF lane that the runtime already
recommended but `pal connect` did not yet expose.

- **New `pal connect llamacpp`.** Added `scripts/connect-llamacpp.ps1`
  and wired it through `pal.ps1`, `pal.json`, `agents.json`, `pal models
  serving`, `pal welcome`, `pal next`, `pal preflight`, `pal benchmark`,
  `CODE_MAP`, `INDEX`, and operator docs. The helper probes `/health` and
  `/v1/models`, prints `/metrics` and `/v1/chat/completions` proof commands,
  and can write `PalLLM:Inference` to `http://localhost:8080/v1/` with a
  stable alias while explicitly setting `ResidencyProvider=Disabled` because
  current `llama-server` residency is controlled by server flags, not a
  chat-completions TTL. `-WireVision` can also point `PalLLM:Vision` at the
  same endpoint after an mmproj proof.
- **GGUF serving hints refreshed to current llama.cpp flags.**
  `ModelCollaborationPlanner` and the docs now name the current
  `llama-server` proof posture: loopback `--host`, `-c`, `-np`, `-b`, `-ub`,
  `-ngl`, `--flash-attn on`, `--metrics`, `--no-webui`, prompt-cache
  `--cache-prompt` / `--cache-reuse` / `-sps` / `-cram`, proof-only
  `-ctk/-ctv` KV compression, `--sleep-idle-seconds` wake-latency proof, and
  `--spec-type` native speculation proof before promotion.
- **Docs and source guards resynced.** `MODEL_COLLABORATION`, `API`,
  `OPERATIONS`, `TUNING`, `READINESS`, `INDEX`, `CODE_MAP`, the feature
  catalog, and meta/source-guard tests now cover the direct llama.cpp lane
  without changing route, MCP, feature, or test counts.
- **Research basis:** current official llama.cpp server README and multimodal
  docs for `/v1` serving, `/health`, `/metrics`, `--sleep-idle-seconds`,
  `-cram`, `-ctk/-ctv`, `-hf`, `--mmproj`, and libmtmd image/audio support.
  A fresh sibling scan under `D:\Coding` found analogous bundled llama-server
  lifecycle and resource-governor ideas in RimLLM, but no code was lifted
  because those paths are target-specific while PalLLM's publishable
  surface stays external-HTTP and Palworld-focused.
- **Verification:** PowerShell parser checks; `connect-llamacpp.ps1`
  write-config dry run; focused `ModelTierTests` + `MetaTests` `35 / 35`;
  `dotnet test` `646 / 646`; full audit `15/15` PASS at
  `artifacts/full-audit/20260506-201538/RESULTS.md`.

### Pass 158 - Schema + Lua-version drift cleanup (2026-05-06)

The "third deep-sweep pass on overnight changes" sweep. Passes 156 + 157
targeted source-tree drift, hardcoded literals, and count mismatches.
This pass swept areas they didn't reach: schema files, JSON `$schema`
references, and version declarations spread across docs that the
drift gates don't directly compare.

- **Lua version drift across five docs.** `agents.json`, `CLAUDE.md`,
  `mod/README.md`, `docs/MENTAL_MODEL.md`, and `docs/PROJECT_NUMBERS.json`
  all said "Lua 5.1" while the authoritative sources -- `.luacheckrc`
  ("UE4SS targets Lua 5.4 on the current public release line"),
  `.github/workflows/lua.yml` ("luacheck (Lua 5.4)"), and
  `PalLlmFeatureCatalog.cs` ("Targets Lua 5.4") -- consistently pin
  Lua 5.4 (UE4SS's current public-release runtime). All five
  out-of-sync docs resynced to 5.4. None of the 15 drift gates
  cross-checks this kind of "same fact in five docs" version drift;
  the scout caught it by directly diffing the files.
- **`docs/schemas/personality-pack.schema.json` rejected its own
  sample packs.** Schema had `"additionalProperties": false` but
  every sample pack (`samples/packs/*/pack.json`) embeds a
  `"$schema": "../../../docs/schemas/personality-pack.schema.json"`
  key for inline editor validation. A strict JSON Schema 2020-12
  validator (e.g. `Test-Json` against this schema) would reject
  every sample. Schema now lists `$schema` as an allowed optional
  string property under `properties`.
- **`docs/PROJECT_NUMBERS.json` referenced a missing schema.** The
  file's `$schema` key pointed at `./schemas/project-numbers.schema.json`
  which had never existed. New `docs/schemas/project-numbers.schema.json`
  (180 lines) ships covering every required field
  (`tests` / `driftGates` / `mcpTools` / `fallbackStrategies` /
  `featureCatalog` / `honestRoadmap` / etc.), the type constraints
  (`tests` is non-negative integer; `driftGates` matches
  `^[0-9]+/[0-9]+$`; `honestRoadmap` matches `^[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?%$`;
  `baselineDate` is `format: date`), the optional nested blocks
  (`runtime`, `linksToSourceOfTruth`, `notes`), and cross-references
  to which audit gate enforces each count.
- **`docs/schemas/README.md` index listed 4 of 8 schemas.** The
  README claimed to be a complete index but `docs/schemas/` actually
  contained 7 schemas (now 8 after this pass). Index rebuilt to
  include `agents.schema.json`, `pal-verbs.schema.json`,
  `install-manifest.schema.json`, and the new
  `project-numbers.schema.json`, each with its source-of-truth
  pointer. README's `Last audited:` stamp also moved 2026-05-05 ->
  2026-05-06.
- **`docs/schemas/pal-verbs.schema.json` `$id` host inconsistency.**
  Used `https://palllm.local/schemas/pal-verbs.schema.json` while
  every other schema in the directory uses `palllm.dev` plus a
  `-v1` version suffix in the path. Resynced to
  `https://palllm.dev/schemas/pal-verbs-v1.schema.json`.
- **`docs/schemas/outbox-envelope.schema.json` `Presentation`
  missing from required.** The schema's docstring said "Always
  present" but the `required` array listed only `RequestId` and
  `AssistantMessage`. INVARIANTS.md guarantee #3 says every
  `ChatResponse` carries a non-null `PresentationCuePlan`, so the
  outbox shape (which wraps `ChatResponse`) inherits that contract.
  Added `"Presentation"` to the required array so the schema
  enforces what the runtime guarantees.
- **`docs/READINESS.md` test count.** Linter race during Pass 157
  left the file stuck at `645 / 645 tests`; bumped to
  `646 / 646` to match the canonical count.

No code changes (the new schema is data + the doc updates are pure
markdown). All seven fixes are pure metadata corrections that close
real correctness gaps a strict JSON-Schema validator or a careful
reader would catch.

Verification: `dotnet test` `646 / 646`; full audit `15/15` PASS at
`artifacts/full-audit/20260506-164618/RESULTS.md`.

### Pass 157 - Second-order drift sweep (2026-05-06)

The "what did Pass 156 miss?" follow-on sweep. Same deep-sweep posture as
Pass 156 but specifically targeting categories the previous scout pair
didn't reach. Three concrete fixes plus a new meta-test that gates a
category that was previously ungated.

- **`docs/SUGGESTIONS.md` heading count off by one.** The heading said
  "Live hint codes (12)" and the "How to add a new hint code" recipe at
  line 218 told contributors to bump `12 -> 13`, but the table only
  listed 11 rows -- matching the 11 `suggestions.Add(...)` calls in
  `HealthSuggestionBuilder.Build()`. Pre-bumped count was a self-bug:
  the doc claimed a code count one ahead of reality. Fixed the heading
  to read `(11)` and the recipe to `11 -> 12`. A future hint code
  addition now follows the documented contract.
- **`src/PalLLM.Sidecar/SelfDescriptionBuilder.cs` hardcoded literals.**
  The file pins three values that the existing regex-based drift gates
  don't reach: `"38 tools, ..."` (the MCP surface descriptor in the
  `pal_describe` payload), `AuditedOn: "2026-05-06"`, and
  `FallbackStrategyCount: 19`. None of these would have failed any of
  the 15 gates if they drifted; they would have silently aged. New
  `SelfDescriptionBuilder_HardcodedLiterals_MatchProjectNumbers`
  meta-test in `tests/PalLLM.Tests/MetaTests.cs` reads
  `docs/PROJECT_NUMBERS.json`'s `mcpTools`, `fallbackStrategies`, and
  `baselineDate` and asserts the C# file contains the matching string
  literals. A future bump to any of those numbers now fails this test
  until the C# file is updated alongside the JSON, closing the
  silent-aging window the same way `Drift_Test_count_docs` already
  gates the markdown-side test count. Test count rolls `645 -> 646`.
- **`docs/MCP_QUICKSTART.md` Step 4 examples** didn't mention the three
  health-aware MCP tools added in Passes 133/140/141 (`pal_status`,
  `pal_health_score`, `pal_health_suggestions`). A fresh operator
  following the doc would never discover them, even though the docs
  for them are extensive elsewhere. Added a natural-prompt example
  bullet pointing at all three so the most-trodden onboarding path
  surfaces the diagnostic surface.

No code changes beyond the new meta-test. Pure correctness sweep that
fixes one outright count-bug, brings three previously-ungated literals
under audit coverage, and closes one MCP-onboarding doc gap.

Verification: `dotnet test` `646 / 646`; full audit `15/15` PASS at
`artifacts/full-audit/20260506-161528/RESULTS.md`.

### Pass 156 - Cross-codebase audit + drift cleanup (2026-05-06)

The "find what the 15 drift gates can't catch" sweep. The drift gates passed
15/15 but I dispatched two parallel deep-sweep scouts to look for stale
docstrings, command-text drift, broken doc links, redundant work in handlers,
and string-coupling issues that compile cleanly but break silently when the
referenced contract moves. Five concrete fixes landed; two NITs deferred.

- **`docs/QUICKREF.md:40` -- broken script path.** The `pack` row referenced
  a `pal-pack-new.ps1` script under `scripts/` which has never existed.
  The real scaffolder is `scripts/scaffold-pack.ps1` (matches `pal.json:50`
  and `pal.ps1:527`).
  Fixed to list the three actually-existing scripts:
  `pal-pack-list.ps1`, `pal-pack-copy.ps1`, `scaffold-pack.ps1`.
- **`scripts/pal-next.ps1` docstring drift.** The `.DESCRIPTION` priority
  list still said `Sidecar up, no packs loaded -> pal pack new ...` even
  though Pass 130 moved the live recommendation to
  `pal pack copy companion-warrior`. Docstring synced. Same fix added an
  `Inference wired but unreachable -> pal doctor` step that the live code
  already implements but the docstring omitted, plus broadened the
  inference-lane line to mention the full set of `pal connect` targets
  (ollama / vllm / omni / foundry / openvino / tensorrt / transformers).
- **`pal.ps1` Run-List `pack` description.** Listed only
  `pal pack list` / `pal pack new` even though the COOKBOOK, the
  `no-packs-loaded` HealthSuggestion, and `pal pack`'s own help text all
  advertise `copy <name>` as the canonical "drop a sample pack" path.
  Description now lists all three subcommands.
- **`src/PalLLM.Sidecar/Program.cs` `/api/why` handler -- double
  GetHealth.** The handler was calling `runtime.GetHealth()` twice in one
  lambda (once inline, once inside the `OperatorHealthScorer.Score(...)`
  argument). This doubled the snapshot-assembly cost on every `/api/why`
  call AND opened a small consistency window where WhyEngine and the
  score it received could see different `RuntimeHealth` snapshots if
  counters ticked between the two reads. Hoisted into a `RuntimeHealth
  health = runtime.GetHealth();` local that feeds both consumers.
- **`src/PalLLM.Sidecar/Mcp/PalLlmMcpTools.cs` `pal_status` -- raw severity
  literals.** The severity-bucket counter was using raw string literals
  (`"urgent"`, `"warn"`, `"info"`) in a `switch` block instead of the
  canonical `HealthSuggestionBuilder.Severity.Urgent / Warn / Info`
  constants that exist for exactly this case. A future builder change
  (capitalisation tweak, new bucket, rename) would have silently
  miscounted. Switched to the constants so the coupling shows at compile
  time. Same pattern the rest of the consumer surface uses.

**NIT findings deliberately deferred:**

- XML doc summaries on `RuntimeHealth` / `LatencyHistogramBucket` /
  `ChatRequest`. These are publication-facing contract types that lack
  class-level `<summary>` blocks. Worth fixing in a documentation pass
  but not a correctness issue.
- `/api/snapshot` POST has no rate-limiter and no body-shape validation.
  The endpoint is gated by `Auth:ApiKey` when configured and runs on
  localhost by default; adding a rate-limiter requires sizing decisions
  and adding validation requires defining what counts as "valid"
  (today, all-default values overwrite the live snapshot). Both need
  design rather than mechanical fix; deferred.

No new code paths, no contract changes, no test additions. Pure drift
cleanup that prevents two correctness footguns and three documentation
mismatches the next contributor would have hit.

Verification: `dotnet test` `645 / 645`; full audit `15/15` PASS at
`artifacts/full-audit/20260506-155609/RESULTS.md`.

### Pass 155 - TensorRT-LLM proof lane + connector (2026-05-06)

The "make the NVIDIA TensorRT-LLM lane explicit and measurable" pass. Current
TensorRT-LLM docs expose `trtllm-serve` as an OpenAI-compatible local server
with `/health`, `/metrics`, `/v1/models`, and `/v1/chat/completions`, plus
YAML-backed controls for KV cache, batching, parsers, speculation,
multimodal requests, and disaggregated-serving topologies. PalLLM now treats
that path as a proof-gated `/v1` lane beside Ollama, vLLM, SGLang, llama.cpp,
`transformers serve`, OpenVINO Model Server, and Foundry Local.

- **Serving profiles now understand TensorRT-LLM.** Non-GGUF text and
  multimodal lanes can name TensorRT-LLM in `PreferredRuntime` and emit
  startup, request, cache, admission, security, and verification guidance for
  `trtllm-serve`, `/health`, `/metrics`, `served_model_name`, config-YAML
  hashes, `--tool_call_parser`, `kvCacheStats`, inflight batching,
  speculation A/B proof, disaggregated serving / Dynamo proof, malformed-media
  fallback, exact JSON/tool-call parse success, and deterministic fallback
  activation.
- **New `pal connect tensorrt`.** Added `scripts/connect-tensorrt.ps1` plus
  `pal.ps1`, `pal.json`, `agents.json`, `pal models serving`, and docs
  coverage. The helper prints bare-metal and Docker setup commands, uses
  loopback for bare metal and container `0.0.0.0` only inside Docker, verifies
  `/health`, `/v1/models`, `/v1/chat/completions`, and `/metrics`, prints a
  proof YAML starter, and can write `PalLLM:Inference` plus optional
  `PalLLM:Vision` with `-WriteConfig`, `-WireVision`, and `-DryRun`.
- **Docs and public-copy scope tightened.** `MODEL_COLLABORATION`, `API`,
  `ARCHITECTURE`, `CODE_MAP`, `INDEX`, `TUNING`, `OPERATIONS`, `READINESS`,
  `BLACKWELL_RECIPES`, `QUANTIZATION`, and the feature catalog now describe
  TensorRT-LLM as a local, loopback-first, proof-gated PalLLM model lane.
  Broad "any local-AI app" wording in current docs was tightened back toward
  PalLLM / Palworld companion scope.

Research basis: current TensorRT-LLM `trtllm-serve`, OpenAI API, metrics,
speculative-decoding, disaggregated-serving, multimodal, and NVIDIA Dynamo
TensorRT-LLM backend docs. The active `D:\Coding` sibling scan (external sibling research) contributed only proof-policy ideas around
metrics receipts, KV-cache caution, and advisory serving lanes; no sibling
code, branding, or identity was copied.

Verification: PowerShell parser check for `connect-tensorrt.ps1`; direct

### Pass 152 - Bridge proof lane checklist + publication-copy cleanup (2026-05-06)

The "make the native proof blocker easier to act on" pass. The next true
roadmap unlock still requires a live Palworld `delivery_proven` run, but the
proof surface no longer makes every consumer rederive the same checklist from
nested readiness and loop fields.

- **Bridge proof now carries normalized lanes.** `GET /api/bridge/proof`
  now includes `ProofLanes[]` with stable lane names, required/optional
  posture, `PASS` / `WARN` / `FAIL` status, summary, and next action for
  `bridge_boot`, `user_widget_compat`, `ui_probe_capture`,
  `native_hud_bind`, `chat_ingress`, `outbox_reply`, `visible_delivery`, and
  `action_feedback`. This gives dashboards, `pal proof`, smoke tooling, and
  future release automation one machine-readable checklist instead of local
  parsing rules.
- **Contract and docs resynced.** The OpenAPI snapshot, API docs,
  architecture notes, and feature-catalog copy now describe the proof-lane
  surface. The endpoint regression test pins the lane names and success
  states on a synthetic bridge -> chat -> outbox -> delivery -> feedback run.
- **Publication wording tightened.** Positive "lawyer-proof" wording was
  removed from code/catalog copy and replaced with compliance/publication
  phrasing. The remaining occurrences are guardrails that explicitly forbid
  making that claim.

Research basis: current UE4SS Lua/API documentation for live hook/widget
workflows, current Microsoft ASP.NET Core/.NET guidance on typed minimal API
contracts and response caching, and an active sibling-project scan. RimLLM's
external-validation packets and an external project's runtime-qualification lanes
reinforced the same idea: native proof should travel as normalized evidence
lanes, not prose-only status.

Verification: focused `SidecarEndpointTests` + `MetaTests` `68 / 68`;
OpenAPI export + `-Verify` PASS; `dotnet test` `645 / 645`; full audit

### Pass 151 - Gemma 3n + Qwen Omni audio proof lanes (2026-05-06)

The "make current local audio/multimodal model lanes honest and usable" pass.
Current Google Gemma docs expose Gemma 3n/Gemma 4 audio input with mono
16 kHz, 30-second clip guidance, Hugging Face `transformers serve` exposes
OpenAI-compatible `input_audio` plus `/v1/audio/transcriptions`, and Qwen
Omni/vLLM-Omni expose local text/image/audio/video understanding plus
isolated audio-output lanes. PalLLM now reflects those facts in the
machine-readable model-serving contract without making speech a hot-path
dependency.

- **Serving profile updated.** `Capability.ServingProfile` now detects
  Gemma 3n model ids as text/image/video/audio-in lanes, emits Gemma 3n
  PLE/conditional-parameter-loading memory guidance, normalizes audio proof
  guidance around local 16 kHz mono clips capped at 30 seconds, and adds
  Qwen Omni-specific vLLM-Omni `--omni`, `modalities=["text","audio"]`,
  speaker/text-mirror, and audio-output proof checks.
- **`pal connect omni` made more usable.** The vLLM-Omni wizard now prefers
  currently usable local ids: `Qwen/Qwen3-Omni-30B-A3B-Instruct`,
  `Qwen/Qwen3-Omni-Flash-Instruct`, `google/gemma-4-*`, and
  `google/gemma-3n-E2B-it`. Qwen3.5-Omni is treated as research-only until
  there is an actual local artifact or provider-compatible endpoint to wire.
  Its no-write result object now reports `DryRun=true` when `-DryRun` is
  supplied even without `-WriteConfig`.
- **Docs resynced.** Model-collaboration, multimodal, architecture,
  readiness, API, handoff, and feature-catalog copy now draw a clear line:
  PalLLM has proof-gated audio-input/audio-output serving lanes, but it still
  does not ship first-party mic capture or a realtime proxy in the chat hot
  path.

Research basis: current Google Gemma audio/Gemma 3n/video docs, Hugging Face
`transformers serve` docs, Qwen3-Omni upstream docs, Qwen3.5-Omni technical
report, and current vLLM-Omni Qwen3-Omni / speech / streaming docs. The active
`D:\Coding` sibling scan contributed only the "normalize media payloads and
pin proof artifacts before promotion" idea; no sibling code, branding, or
identity was copied.

### Pass 149 - vLLM interactivity proof lane + README count cleanup (2026-05-06)

The "make the current low-latency vLLM knob visible but proof-gated" pass.
Current vLLM serve arguments expose `--performance-mode interactivity` for
small-batch interactive traffic. PalLLM now surfaces that as a measured
player-facing serving-lane candidate instead of leaving it buried in external
docs.

- **Machine-readable serving profile updated.** vLLM-capable
  `Capability.ServingProfile` lanes now include
  `--performance-mode interactivity` startup guidance for companion, vision,
  and narration endpoints where player-facing p95 latency matters. The cache
  and verification sections explicitly require comparing interactivity against
  `balanced` / `throughput` on the same PalLLM replay set before changing a
  shared server default.
- **`pal connect vllm` emits the low-latency default.** The vLLM connect wizard
  now assigns `PerformanceMode=interactivity` for the companion, vision, and
  narration use cases and includes `--performance-mode interactivity` in the
  generated Docker command. Coding/productivity recipes stay on vLLM's balanced
  default unless the operator changes them.
- **Docs and feature catalog resynced.** `docs/MODEL_COLLABORATION.md`,
  `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`, `docs/BLACKWELL_RECIPES.md`, and the
  `hardware-aware-model-collaboration` catalog entry now explain the measured
  mode boundary. `docs/BLACKWELL_RECIPES.md` was also tightened back toward
  PalLLM/Palworld companion scope instead of broad "any local-AI app" wording.
- **Publication count cleanup.** One README status block still said `36` MCP
  tools despite the live surface and other audited docs saying `38`. It now
  matches the current MCP surface.

Research basis: current official vLLM serve-argument docs for
`--performance-mode`, plus current primary Gemma, Qwen Omni, Ollama,
llama.cpp, SGLang, and ONNX Runtime docs reviewed for adjacent local-model
ideas. The active `D:\Coding` sibling scan reinforced model decisions as
